title: Mährchen von einem, der auszog das Fürchten zu lernen
titleEn: The Story of a Youth Who Went Forth to Learn What Fear Was
part: The Story of a Youth Who Went Forth to Learn What Fear Was
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 4. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: KHM 4, the long comic-eerie tale of a boy too dense to feel fear, who sets out to ‘learn what shuddering is.’ He spends a night beneath the gallows and then three nights in a haunted castle — monstrous cats, a bed that gallops, ninepins played with dead men’s bones and skulls, a corpse in a coffin, a bearded giant at the forge — and wins a princess without ever once shuddering; only his wife’s bucketful of cold water and wriggling gudgeons finally teaches him. The running joke turns on ‘es gruselt mir’ and ‘das Gruseln’ — to shudder, to feel the creeps — which the tale treats as a skill to be learned. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, gieng, fieng, thun/gethan, Thurm, Noth, todt, funfzig and gescheidt.

sentences.t: Ein Vater hatte zwei Söhne, davon war der älteste klug und gescheidt, und wußte sich in alles wohl zu schicken, der jüngste aber war dumm, konnte nichts begreifen und lernen:
sentences.l: A father had two sons, of whom the eldest was clever and shrewd, and knew well how to adapt himself to everything, but the youngest was dumb, could grasp and learn nothing:
sentences.i: A father had two sons; the elder was clever and shrewd and knew well how to turn his hand to anything, but the younger was dumb and could neither grasp nor learn a thing.
sentences.n: “davon war der älteste” — davon (‘of them’) points back to the two sons; German loves such demonstrative adverbs. “sich in alles schicken” is an idiom, ‘to adapt oneself to anything, make the best of things.’ gescheidt is the 1857 spelling of gescheit (‘clever’).

sentences.t: und wenn ihn die Leute sahen, sprachen sie „mit dem wird der Vater noch seine Last haben!“
sentences.l: and when the people saw him, they said, “with that one the father will yet have his burden!”
sentences.i: and whenever people saw him, they said, “The father will have his hands full with that one!”
sentences.n: “mit dem wird der Vater noch seine Last haben” — literally ‘with that one the father will yet have his burden,’ i.e. he will be nothing but trouble. The demonstrative dem (‘that one’) is faintly dismissive.

sentences.t: Wenn nun etwas zu thun war, so mußte es der älteste allzeit ausrichten:
sentences.l: When now something was to do, then the eldest had always to carry it out:
sentences.i: Now whenever there was something to be done, the eldest always had to see to it;
sentences.n: thun is the 1857 spelling of tun (‘to do’); the old -th- recurs throughout (gethan, aufgethan, zuthun). allzeit = ‘always, at all times’ (archaic). ausrichten here = to carry out, accomplish.

sentences.t: hieß ihn aber der Vater noch spät oder gar in der Nacht etwas holen, und der Weg gieng dabei über den Kirchhof oder sonst einen schaurigen Ort, so antwortete er wohl „ach nein, Vater, ich gehe nicht dahin, es gruselt mir!“ denn er fürchtete sich.
sentences.l: but did the father bid him fetch something late or even in the night, and the way went thereby over the churchyard or some other eerie place, then he would answer, “oh no, father, I do not go there, it shudders me!” for he was afraid.
sentences.i: but if the father bade him fetch something late in the evening, or even at night, and the road ran past the churchyard or some other eerie spot, he would answer, “Oh no, father, I won’t go there — it gives me the creeps!” for he was afraid.
sentences.n: This is the tale’s engine. “es gruselt mir” — gruseln is an impersonal verb, literally ‘it shudders to me,’ i.e. ‘I get the creeps, I shudder with dread.’ The whole story turns on the boy who cannot feel this; “das Fürchten / das Gruseln lernen” means ‘to learn what fear / shuddering is.’ You will meet gruseln in every shape — gruselt, gruselte, gruselts, gegruselt, das Gruseln — so fix it now. “hieß ihn ... holen”: heißen + infinitive = to bid, order someone to do something. gieng is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’).

sentences.t: Oder, wenn Abends beim Feuer Geschichten erzählt wurden, wobei einem die Haut schaudert, so sprachen die Zuhörer manchmal „ach, es gruselt mir!“
sentences.l: Or, when in the evening by the fire stories were told, at which one’s skin shudders, then the listeners sometimes said, “oh, it shudders me!”
sentences.i: Or when tales were told of an evening by the fire, the kind that make your skin crawl, the listeners would sometimes say, “Oh, it gives me the creeps!”
sentences.n: “wobei einem die Haut schaudert” — einem is the dative of the indefinite ‘one’ (‘one’s skin shudders’); schaudern is a near-twin of gruseln, ‘to shudder.’ Abends (‘of an evening’) is an adverbial genitive.

sentences.t: Der jüngste saß in einer Ecke und hörte das mit an, und konnte nicht begreifen was es heißen sollte.
sentences.l: The youngest sat in a corner and listened to that along, and could not grasp what it was supposed to mean.
sentences.i: The youngest sat in a corner and listened, and could not make out what it was supposed to mean.
sentences.n: “hörte das mit an” — the separable mit anhören, ‘to listen in.’ “was es heißen sollte” = what it was meant to mean (heißen here = ‘to mean, signify’).

sentences.t: „Immer sagen sie es gruselt mir! es gruselt mir! mir gruselts nicht: das wird wohl eine Kunst sein, von der ich auch nichts verstehe.“
sentences.l: “Always they say, it shudders me! it shudders me! To me it shudders not: that will surely be an art of which I too understand nothing.”
sentences.i: “They’re forever saying, it gives me the creeps! it gives me the creeps! It gives me no creeps at all — it must be some art I’ve no notion of either.”
sentences.n: “mir gruselts nicht” = gruselt es nicht (‘it doesn’t shudder me’), the contracted -s standing for es. “eine Kunst” here = a skill, an accomplishment — the boy takes fear to be a craft one can learn, which is the tale’s whole conceit.

sentences.t: Nun geschah es, daß der Vater einmal zu ihm sprach „hör du, in der Ecke dort, du wirst groß und stark, du mußt auch etwas lernen womit du dein Brot verdienst.
sentences.l: Now it happened that the father once said to him, “listen, you there in the corner, you are growing big and strong, you must also learn something with which you earn your bread.
sentences.i: Now it happened that the father once said to him, “Listen, you in the corner there — you’re growing big and strong; you too must learn some trade to earn your bread by.
sentences.n: “hör du” — a blunt ‘listen, you.’ “womit du dein Brot verdienst” = ‘by which you earn your bread,’ i.e. a livelihood. daß is the 1857 spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Siehst du, wie dein Bruder sich Mühe gibt, aber an dir ist Hopfen und Malz verloren.“
sentences.l: Do you see how your brother takes pains, but on you are hops and malt lost.”
sentences.i: You see how your brother works at it — but with you it’s all wasted effort.”
sentences.n: “an dir ist Hopfen und Malz verloren” is a set idiom from brewing — ‘the hops and malt are wasted on you,’ i.e. you’re a hopeless case, nothing can be made of you. “sich Mühe geben” = to take pains, make an effort.

sentences.t: „Ei, Vater,“ antwortete er, „ich will gerne was lernen; ja, wenns angienge, so möchte ich lernen daß mirs gruselte; davon verstehe ich noch gar nichts.“
sentences.l: “Eh, father,” he answered, “I will gladly learn something; yes, if it were possible, then I should like to learn that it might shudder me; of that I understand as yet nothing at all.”
sentences.i: “Why, father,” he answered, “I’d gladly learn something; indeed, if it could be managed, I should like to learn to shudder — I’ve no notion of it at all yet.”
sentences.n: “wenns angienge” = wenn es anginge, subjunctive II of angehen, ‘if it were feasible’ (angienge is the 1857 spelling). “daß mirs gruselte” — again the impersonal gruseln in the past subjunctive, ‘that it might give me the creeps.’ möchte (‘should like’) is the polite subjunctive of mögen.

sentences.t: Der älteste lachte als er das hörte, und dachte bei sich „du lieber Gott, was ist mein Bruder ein Dummbart, aus dem wird sein Lebtag nichts: was ein Häckchen werden will, muß sich bei Zeiten krümmen.“
sentences.l: The eldest laughed when he heard that, and thought to himself, “dear God, what a blockhead my brother is; out of him will come nothing his life long: what wants to become a little hook must bend itself betimes.”
sentences.i: The eldest laughed to hear it, and thought to himself, “Good Lord, what a dolt my brother is; nothing will ever come of him as long as he lives — what’s to grow into a hook must start bending early.”
sentences.n: “aus dem wird ... nichts” = ‘nothing will become of him.’ Lebtag = ‘(one’s) livelong day,’ i.e. ever. The proverb “was ein Häckchen werden will, muß sich bei Zeiten krümmen” (Häckchen = little hook; 1857 for Häkchen) is the German ‘as the twig is bent, so grows the tree.’ Dummbart literally = ‘dumb-beard,’ a dolt.

sentences.t: Der Vater seufzte und antwortete ihm „das Gruseln, das sollst du schon lernen, aber dein Brot wirst du damit nicht verdienen.“
sentences.l: The father sighed and answered him, “the shuddering, that you shall indeed learn, but your bread you will not earn with it.”
sentences.i: The father sighed and answered him, “The shuddering — that you shall learn right enough, but you’ll never earn your bread by it.”
sentences.n: “das Gruseln” — here gruseln is nominalized (‘the shuddering, the creeps’), the very thing the boy wants to learn. The fronted “das Gruseln, das ...” repeats it with a resumptive das for emphasis. schon here = ‘right enough, surely.’

sentences.t: Bald danach kam der Küster zum Besuch ins Haus, da klagte ihm der Vater seine Noth und erzählte wie sein jüngster Sohn in allen Dingen so schlecht beschlagen wäre, er wüßte nichts und lernte nichts.
sentences.l: Soon after, the sexton came on a visit into the house; there the father lamented to him his distress and told how his youngest son was so ill-versed in all things, that he knew nothing and learned nothing.
sentences.i: Soon afterward the sexton came to the house on a visit, and the father poured out his troubles to him, telling how his youngest son was so poorly equipped in everything — he knew nothing and learned nothing.
sentences.n: der Küster = the sexton (the church officer who rings the bells and tends the building). Noth is the 1857 spelling of Not (‘distress, trouble’), with the old -th-. “schlecht beschlagen” = poorly versed, ill-equipped (beschlagen, literally ‘shod,’ used of knowledge). The reported speech goes into the subjunctive — wäre, wüßte, lernte — marking it as the father’s account.

sentences.t: „Denkt euch, als ich ihn fragte, womit er sein Brot verdienen wollte, hat er gar verlangt das Gruseln zu lernen.“
sentences.l: “Just imagine, when I asked him with what he wanted to earn his bread, he actually demanded to learn the shuddering.”
sentences.i: “Just imagine — when I asked him what he meant to earn his bread by, he actually asked to learn how to shudder!”
sentences.n: “Denkt euch” = ‘just imagine’ (imperative of sich denken). gar here is intensifying, ‘actually, of all things.’ verlangen = to demand, ask for.

sentences.t: „Wenns weiter nichts ist,“ antwortete der Küster, „das kann er bei mir lernen; thut ihn nur zu mir, ich will ihn schon abhobeln.“
sentences.l: “If it is nothing further,” answered the sexton, “that he can learn with me; just put him to me, I will surely plane him down.”
sentences.i: “If that’s all,” answered the sexton, “he can learn it with me; just hand him over to me, and I’ll knock the rough edges off him.”
sentences.n: “wenns weiter nichts ist” = ‘if that’s all there is to it.’ thut is the 1857 spelling of tut (imperative of thun/tun); “thut ihn zu mir” = put/send him to me. abhobeln = literally ‘to plane down’ (like a carpenter smoothing wood), figuratively to lick into shape.

sentences.t: Der Vater war es zufrieden, weil er dachte „der Junge wird doch ein wenig zugestutzt.“
sentences.l: The father was content with it, because he thought, “the boy will at least be trimmed a little.”
sentences.i: The father was content with that, thinking, “At least the boy will get licked into shape a bit.”
sentences.n: “war es zufrieden” — zufrieden here takes es (‘content with it’). zustutzen = to trim, prune, lick into shape (participle zugestutzt). doch here = ‘at least, after all.’

sentences.t: Der Küster nahm ihn also ins Haus, und er mußte die Glocke läuten.
sentences.l: The sexton took him thus into the house, and he had to ring the bell.
sentences.i: So the sexton took him into his house, and he had to ring the bell.
sentences.n: läuten = to ring or toll (a bell); ringing the church bell is the sexton’s work.

sentences.t: Nach ein paar Tagen weckte er ihn um Mitternacht, hieß ihn aufstehen, in den Kirchthurm steigen und läuten.
sentences.l: After a couple of days he woke him at midnight, bade him get up, climb into the church-tower and ring.
sentences.i: After a few days he woke him at midnight, bidding him get up, climb the church tower, and ring the bell.
sentences.n: Kirchthurm is the 1857 spelling of Kirchturm (‘church tower, steeple’), keeping both the old -th- and the -thurm of Thurm. One heißen (‘bade’) governs the three infinitives aufstehen, steigen, läuten.

sentences.t: „Du sollst schon lernen was Gruseln ist,“ dachte er, gieng heimlich voraus, und als der Junge oben war, und sich umdrehte und das Glockenseil fassen wollte, so sah er auf der Treppe, dem Schallloch gegenüber, eine weiße Gestalt stehen.
sentences.l: “You shall indeed learn what shuddering is,” he thought, went secretly ahead, and when the boy was up top, and turned round and wanted to seize the bell-rope, then he saw on the stairs, opposite the sound-hole, a white figure standing.
sentences.i: “You shall learn what shuddering is, right enough,” he thought, and slipped up ahead in secret; and when the boy was at the top and turned to take hold of the bell-rope, he saw a white figure standing on the stairs, opposite the sound-hole.
sentences.n: The sexton (er) means to scare the boy into feeling fear. voraus = ahead, in advance. das Schallloch = the sound-hole, the louvred belfry opening; gegenüber (‘opposite’) takes the dative and here follows its noun. das Glockenseil = the bell-rope.

sentences.t: „Wer da?“ rief er, aber die Gestalt gab keine Antwort, regte und bewegte sich nicht.
sentences.l: “Who there?” he called, but the figure gave no answer, stirred and moved itself not.
sentences.i: “Who’s there?” he cried, but the figure gave no answer and neither stirred nor moved.
sentences.n: “Wer da?” is the old sentry’s challenge, ‘who goes there?’ “sich regen” and “sich bewegen” are both reflexive, ‘to stir, to move.’

sentences.t: „Gib Antwort,“ rief der Junge, „oder mache daß du fort kommst, du hast hier in der Nacht nichts zu schaffen.“
sentences.l: “Give answer,” called the boy, “or see that you get away; you have nothing to do here in the night.”
sentences.i: “Answer me,” cried the boy, “or be off with you — you’ve no business here at night.”
sentences.n: “mache daß du fort kommst” = ‘see to it that you get away,’ a brusque ‘clear off.’ “nichts zu schaffen haben” = to have no business (somewhere).

sentences.t: Der Küster aber blieb unbeweglich stehen, damit der Junge glauben sollte es wäre ein Gespenst.
sentences.l: The sexton, however, remained standing motionless, so that the boy should believe it were a ghost.
sentences.i: But the sexton stood stock-still, so that the boy should think it was a ghost.
sentences.n: unbeweglich = motionless. damit = ‘so that’ (purpose), sending its verb to the end. “es wäre ein Gespenst” — the subjunctive wäre marks what the boy is meant to believe.

sentences.t: Der Junge rief zum zweitenmal „was willst du hier? sprich, wenn du ein ehrlicher Kerl bist, oder ich werfe dich die Treppe hinab.“
sentences.l: The boy called for the second time, “what do you want here? speak, if you are an honest fellow, or I throw you down the stairs.”
sentences.i: The boy called out a second time, “What do you want here? Speak, if you’re an honest fellow, or I’ll throw you down the stairs.”
sentences.n: ehrlicher Kerl = an honest fellow. “die Treppe hinab” = down the stairs (accusative of the path traversed). The threefold challenge — three calls before he acts — is the tale’s folk pattern.

sentences.t: Der Küster dachte „das wird so schlimm nicht gemeint sein,“ gab keinen Laut von sich und stand als wenn er von Stein wäre.
sentences.l: The sexton thought, “that will not be meant so badly,” gave no sound from himself and stood as if he were of stone.
sentences.i: The sexton thought, “He can’t mean it so seriously,” gave not a sound, and stood as though he were made of stone.
sentences.n: “das wird so schlimm nicht gemeint sein” — a confident guess (‘surely he doesn’t mean it that badly’); wird ... sein here expresses probability. “als wenn er von Stein wäre” = as if he were of stone (subjunctive of comparison).

sentences.t: Da rief ihn der Junge zum drittenmale an, und als das auch vergeblich war, nahm er einen Anlauf und stieß das Gespenst die Treppe hinab, daß es zehn Stufen hinab fiel und in einer Ecke liegen blieb.
sentences.l: Then the boy called to him for the third time, and when that too was in vain, he took a run-up and shoved the ghost down the stairs, so that it fell down ten steps and stayed lying in a corner.
sentences.i: Then the boy challenged him a third time, and when that too was in vain, he took a run and shoved the ghost down the stairs, so that it tumbled down ten steps and lay still in a corner.
sentences.n: “rief ihn an” — anrufen (separable), here ‘to call to, challenge.’ zum drittenmale — the decisive third time. “einen Anlauf nehmen” = to take a run-up. The result-clause daß (‘so that it fell...’) is very common in Grimm.

sentences.t: Darauf läutete er die Glocke, gieng heim, legte sich, ohne ein Wort zu sagen, ins Bett und schlief fort.
sentences.l: Thereupon he rang the bell, went home, laid himself, without saying a word, into bed and slept on.
sentences.i: Then he rang the bell, went home, and, without a word, lay down in bed and slept on.
sentences.n: “schlief fort” — fortschlafen, to go on sleeping. The boy’s utter unconcern is the joke: he has just thrown a ‘ghost’ downstairs and calmly goes to bed.

sentences.t: Die Küsterfrau wartete lange Zeit auf ihren Mann, aber er wollte nicht wieder kommen.
sentences.l: The sexton’s wife waited a long time for her husband, but he would not come again.
sentences.i: The sexton’s wife waited a long while for her husband, but he did not come back.
sentences.n: Küsterfrau = the sexton’s wife. “wollte nicht wieder kommen” — here wollen shades toward ‘would not, failed to’ rather than active refusal.

sentences.t: Da ward ihr endlich angst, sie weckte den Jungen, und fragte „weißt du nicht, wo mein Mann geblieben ist? er ist vor dir auf den Thurm gestiegen.“
sentences.l: Then she at last grew afraid, she woke the boy, and asked, “do you not know where my husband has got to? he climbed up the tower before you.”
sentences.i: At last she grew frightened, woke the boy, and asked, “Do you not know where my husband has got to? He went up the tower before you.”
sentences.n: ward is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became’; modern wurde). “ihr ward angst” = ‘fear came over her’ (impersonal, with the dative ihr). “wo ... geblieben ist” = where he has got to. Thurm is the 1857 spelling of Turm (‘tower’).

sentences.t: „Nein,“ antwortete der Junge, „aber da hat einer dem Schallloch gegenüber auf der Treppe gestanden, und weil er keine Antwort geben und auch nicht weggehen wollte, so habe ich ihn für einen Spitzbuben gehalten und hinunter gestoßen.
sentences.l: “No,” answered the boy, “but there someone stood opposite the sound-hole on the stairs, and because he would neither give an answer nor go away, I took him for a rogue and shoved him down.
sentences.i: “No,” answered the boy, “but someone was standing on the stairs opposite the sound-hole, and because he would neither answer nor go away, I took him for a scoundrel and shoved him down.
sentences.n: “einer” = ‘someone, a fellow.’ Spitzbube = a rogue, scoundrel. “für einen Spitzbuben halten” = to take (someone) for a rogue (halten für = to consider as). weggehen (separable) = to go away.

sentences.t: Geht nur hin, so werdet Ihr sehen ob ers gewesen ist, es sollte mir leid thun.“
sentences.l: Just go there, then you will see whether it was he; it should grieve me.”
sentences.i: Just go and look, and you’ll see whether it was he; I’d be sorry for it.”
sentences.n: “geht nur hin” = ‘just go along there’ (hingehen). Ihr (capitalized) is the respectful ‘you’ the boy uses to the sexton’s wife. “ers” = er es (‘whether it was he’). “es sollte mir leid thun” = I’d be sorry (leidtun, with the old thun spelling).

sentences.t: Die Frau sprang fort, und fand ihren Mann, der in einer Ecke lag und jammerte, und ein Bein gebrochen hatte.
sentences.l: The woman sprang away, and found her husband, who lay in a corner and moaned, and had broken a leg.
sentences.i: The woman ran off and found her husband, who lay in a corner moaning, with a broken leg.
sentences.n: “sprang fort” — fortspringen, to dash off. jammern = to wail, moan. “ein Bein gebrochen hatte” = had broken a leg.

sentences.t: Sie trug ihn herab und eilte dann mit lautem Geschrei zu dem Vater des Jungen.
sentences.l: She carried him down and then hurried with loud outcry to the father of the boy.
sentences.i: She carried him down and then hurried, crying loudly, to the boy’s father.
sentences.n: “trug ... herab” — herabtragen, to carry down. “mit lautem Geschrei” = with loud shouting, an outcry.

sentences.t: „Euer Junge,“ rief sie, „hat ein großes Unglück angerichtet, meinen Mann hat er die Treppe hinab geworfen daß er ein Bein gebrochen hat: schafft den Taugenichts aus unserm Hause.“
sentences.l: “Your boy,” she cried, “has caused a great misfortune; my husband he has thrown down the stairs, so that he has broken a leg: get the good-for-nothing out of our house.”
sentences.i: “Your boy,” she cried, “has done a great mischief — he’s thrown my husband down the stairs and broken his leg! Get the good-for-nothing out of our house!”
sentences.n: “ein Unglück anrichten” = to cause a disaster (anrichten, separable). Taugenichts = a good-for-nothing, a ne’er-do-well (literally ‘is-good-for-nothing’). schafft here = imperative of schaffen in the sense ‘get (him) away, remove.’ “unserm Hause” has the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Der Vater erschrack, kam herbeigelaufen und schalt den Jungen aus.
sentences.l: The father took fright, came running up and scolded the boy out.
sentences.i: The father was alarmed, came running up, and scolded the boy roundly.
sentences.n: erschrack is the 1857/archaic preterite of erschrecken (‘took fright’; modern erschrak). “schalt ... aus” — ausschelten, to scold, tell off (schalt is the preterite of schelten).

sentences.t: „Was sind das für gottlose Streiche, die muß dir der Böse eingegeben haben.“
sentences.l: “What sort of godless pranks are these? the Evil One must have put them into you.”
sentences.i: “What godless tricks are these? The Devil himself must have put them into your head.”
sentences.n: “was sind das für ...” = ‘what sort of ... are these?’ (a fixed pattern with für). Streiche = pranks, tricks (singular Streich). der Böse = the Evil One, the Devil. eingeben (separable) = to put into someone’s mind, prompt, inspire.

sentences.t: „Vater,“ antwortete er, „hört nur an, ich bin ganz unschuldig: er stand da in der Nacht, wie einer der böses im Sinne hat.
sentences.l: “Father,” he answered, “just listen, I am quite innocent: he stood there in the night, like one who has evil in mind.
sentences.i: “Father,” he answered, “only listen — I am quite innocent: he stood there in the night like a man with evil intentions.
sentences.n: “hört nur an” — anhören, ‘just hear me out’ (with the respectful Ihr-imperative). unschuldig = innocent. “böses im Sinne haben” = to have evil in mind, to be up to no good (Sinn = mind).

sentences.t: Ich wußte nicht wers war, und habe ihn dreimal ermahnt zu reden oder wegzugehen.“
sentences.l: I did not know who it was, and admonished him three times to speak or to go away.”
sentences.i: I didn’t know who it was, and warned him three times to speak or be gone.”
sentences.n: “wers” = wer es (‘who it was’). ermahnen = to admonish, urge, warn. wegzugehen = the zu-infinitive of weggehen (separable), ‘to go away.’ Note the threefold warning again.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sprach der Vater, „mit dir erleb ich nur Unglück, geh mir aus den Augen, ich will dich nicht mehr ansehen.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the father, “with you I experience only misfortune; get out of my sight, I will look at you no more.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the father, “nothing but misfortune do I have from you — out of my sight! I’ll not set eyes on you again.”
sentences.n: erleb = erlebe (‘I experience, live to see’). “geh mir aus den Augen” = ‘get out of my sight’ (with the ethical dative mir). ansehen (separable) = to look at.

sentences.t: „Ja, Vater, recht gerne, wartet nur bis Tag ist, da will ich ausgehen und das Gruseln lernen, so versteh ich doch eine Kunst, die mich ernähren kann.“
sentences.l: “Yes, father, right gladly; just wait until it is day, then I will go out and learn the shuddering; then I understand at least one art that can feed me.”
sentences.i: “Yes, father, gladly; only wait till daybreak, and I’ll set out and learn to shudder — then at least I’ll know one trade that can keep me fed.”
sentences.n: wartet = the respectful Ihr-imperative (‘wait’). ausgehen (separable) = to go out, set forth. versteh = verstehe; “eine Kunst verstehen” = to know a craft or skill. The boy again treats das Gruseln as a marketable trade.

sentences.t: „Lerne was du willst,“ sprach der Vater, „mir ist alles einerlei.
sentences.l: “Learn what you will,” said the father, “to me it is all one.
sentences.i: “Learn what you like,” said the father; “it’s all the same to me.
sentences.n: “mir ist alles einerlei” = ‘it’s all one to me, I don’t care’ (einerlei = all the same, with the dative mir).

sentences.t: Da hast du funfzig Thaler, damit geh in die weite Welt und sage keinem Menschen wo du her bist und wer dein Vater ist, denn ich muß mich deiner schämen.“
sentences.l: There you have fifty thalers; with that go into the wide world and tell no person where you are from and who your father is, for I must be ashamed of you.”
sentences.i: Here are fifty thalers; go out into the wide world with them, and tell no one where you come from or who your father is, for I am ashamed of you.”
sentences.n: funfzig is the 1857 spelling of fünfzig (‘fifty’); the Thaler was the old silver coin (and the source of ‘dollar’). “sich jemandes schämen” = to be ashamed of someone, with the genitive deiner (‘of you’).

sentences.t: „Ja, Vater, wie ihrs haben wollt, wenn ihr nicht mehr verlangt, das kann ich leicht in Acht behalten.“
sentences.l: “Yes, father, as you will have it; if you demand nothing more, that I can easily keep in mind.”
sentences.i: “Yes, father, as you wish; if that’s all you ask, I can easily keep to it.”
sentences.n: “wie ihrs haben wollt” = wie ihr es haben wollt (‘as you want it’). “in Acht behalten” = to bear in mind, keep to — a cousin of the idiom sich in Acht nehmen.

sentences.t: Als nun der Tag anbrach, steckte der Junge seine funfzig Thaler in die Tasche, gieng hinaus auf die große Landstraße und sprach immer vor sich hin „wenn mirs nur gruselte! wenn mirs nur gruselte!“
sentences.l: When now the day broke, the boy stuck his fifty thalers into his pocket, went out onto the great highroad and kept saying to himself, “if only it shuddered me! if only it shuddered me!”
sentences.i: When day broke, the boy stuffed his fifty thalers into his pocket, went out onto the great highroad, and kept muttering to himself, “If only I could shudder! If only I could shudder!”
sentences.n: “der Tag brach an” — anbrechen, ‘to break, dawn.’ Landstraße = the country highroad. “vor sich hin sprechen” = to say to oneself, to mutter; this refrain “wenn mirs nur gruselte!” becomes the boy’s constant tag-line.

sentences.t: Da kam ein Mann heran, der hörte das Gespräch, das der Junge mit sich selber führte, und als sie ein Stück weiter waren, daß man den Galgen sehen konnte, sagte der Mann zu ihm „siehst du, dort ist der Baum, wo siebene mit des Seilers Tochter Hochzeit gehalten haben und jetzt das Fliegen lernen: setz dich darunter und warte bis die Nacht kommt, so wirst du schon das Gruseln lernen.“
sentences.l: Then a man came up, who heard the conversation that the boy was carrying on with himself, and when they were a stretch further, so that one could see the gallows, the man said to him, “do you see, there is the tree where seven have held their wedding with the rope-maker’s daughter and are now learning to fly: sit down under it and wait till night comes, then you will surely learn what shuddering is.”
sentences.i: Then a man came up who overheard the conversation the boy was holding with himself; and when they had gone a way further, so that the gallows came into view, the man said to him, “See there — that’s the tree where seven have wedded the rope-maker’s daughter and are now learning to fly; sit down beneath it and wait till nightfall, and you’ll learn to shudder, right enough.”
sentences.n: A grim joke: the ‘tree’ is the gallows, the seven are hanged men. “mit des Seilers Tochter Hochzeit gehalten” — the ‘rope-maker’s daughter’ (des Seilers Tochter, genitive) is the noose; ‘they’ve wedded her and are learning to fly’ = they have been hanged and dangle in the wind. Grimm keeps the gallows-humour undiluted. siebene = an old inflected ‘seven (of them).’

sentences.t: „Wenn weiter nichts dazu gehört,“ antwortete der Junge, „das ist leicht gethan; lerne ich aber so geschwind das Gruseln, so sollst du meine funfzig Thaler haben: komm nur Morgen früh wieder zu mir.“
sentences.l: “If nothing further belongs to it,” answered the boy, “that is easily done; but if I learn the shuddering so quickly, then you shall have my fifty thalers: just come back to me tomorrow early.”
sentences.i: “If that’s all it takes,” answered the boy, “it’s easily done; but if I learn to shudder as quickly as that, you shall have my fifty thalers — just come back to me early tomorrow.”
sentences.n: “wenn weiter nichts dazu gehört” = ‘if nothing more is needed for it’ (dazugehören). gethan is the 1857 spelling of getan (‘done’). geschwind = quickly, fast.

sentences.t: Da gieng der Junge zu dem Galgen, setzte sich darunter und wartete bis der Abend kam.
sentences.l: Then the boy went to the gallows, sat down under it and waited until the evening came.
sentences.i: So the boy went to the gallows, sat down beneath it, and waited till evening came.
sentences.n: der Galgen = the gallows. darunter = ‘under it,’ pointing back to the gallows-tree.

sentences.t: Und weil ihn fror, machte er sich ein Feuer an: aber um Mitternacht gieng der Wind so kalt, daß er trotz des Feuers nicht warm werden wollte.
sentences.l: And because he was cold, he made himself a fire: but at midnight the wind blew so cold that, despite the fire, he would not grow warm.
sentences.i: And because he was cold, he lit himself a fire; but at midnight the wind blew so cold that, for all the fire, he could not get warm.
sentences.n: “ihn fror” — the impersonal frieren with an accusative person, ‘he was cold’ (literally ‘it froze him’). “machte ... an” — anmachen, to light, kindle (separable). trotz (‘in spite of’) takes the genitive: des Feuers.

sentences.t: Und als der Wind die Gehenkten gegen einander stieß, daß sie sich hin und her bewegten, so dachte er „du frierst unten bei dem Feuer, was mögen die da oben erst frieren und zappeln.“
sentences.l: And when the wind knocked the hanged men against one another, so that they moved to and fro, he thought, “you are freezing down here by the fire — how those up there must be freezing and dangling all the more.”
sentences.i: And when the wind knocked the hanged men against one another, so that they swung to and fro, he thought, “Here you are freezing by the fire — how much more must those up there be freezing and dangling!”
sentences.n: die Gehenkten = the hanged men (from henken, to hang). “hin und her” = to and fro. “was mögen die ... erst frieren” — mögen here expresses a wondering supposition (‘how they must be freezing’), and erst = ‘all the more.’ zappeln = to wriggle, dangle, jerk about — the tale’s recurring word for helpless twitching.

sentences.t: Und weil er mitleidig war, legte er die Leiter an, stieg hinauf, knüpfte einen nach dem andern los, und holte sie alle siebene herab.
sentences.l: And because he was compassionate, he set the ladder up, climbed up, untied one after the other, and brought them all seven down.
sentences.i: And being tender-hearted, he set up the ladder, climbed up, untied them one after another, and brought all seven down.
sentences.n: mitleidig = compassionate, pitying. “legte ... an” — anlegen (a ladder), to set/place against. “knüpfte ... los” — losknüpfen, to untie (separable). His pity is the comic point: he treats corpses as cold guests.

sentences.t: Darauf schürte er das Feuer, blies es an und setzte sie rings herum, daß sie sich wärmen sollten.
sentences.l: Thereupon he stoked the fire, blew on it and set them round about, so that they should warm themselves.
sentences.i: Then he stirred up the fire, blew on it, and set them all around it, so that they might warm themselves.
sentences.n: schüren = to stoke, poke (a fire); “blies ... an” — anblasen, to blow on (to kindle). “rings herum” = round about. “sich wärmen” = to warm oneself.

sentences.t: Aber sie saßen da und regten sich nicht, und das Feuer ergriff ihre Kleider.
sentences.l: But they sat there and did not stir, and the fire caught their clothes.
sentences.i: But they sat there and never stirred, and the fire caught their clothes.
sentences.n: “sich regen” = to stir, move. “das Feuer ergriff ihre Kleider” — ergreifen here ‘to catch, take hold of’ (the flames catch their garments).

sentences.t: Da sprach er „nehmt euch in Acht, sonst häng ich euch wieder hinauf.“
sentences.l: Then he said, “take heed, or else I hang you up again.”
sentences.i: Then he said, “Take care, or I’ll hang you up again.”
sentences.n: “sich in Acht nehmen” = to take care, be on one’s guard. häng = hänge (‘I hang’). The absurdity — scolding corpses for letting their rags burn — is played perfectly straight.

sentences.t: Die Todten aber hörten nicht, schwiegen und ließen ihre Lumpen fort brennen.
sentences.l: The dead, however, did not hear, kept silent and let their rags go on burning.
sentences.i: But the dead did not hear; they kept silent and let their rags burn on.
sentences.n: Todten is the 1857 spelling of Toten (‘the dead’; compare todt = tot below). Lumpen = rags, tatters. “ließen ... fort brennen” — fortbrennen, to go on burning (fort = ‘on, away’).

sentences.t: Da ward er bös und sprach „wenn ihr nicht Acht geben wollt, so kann ich euch nicht helfen, ich will nicht mit euch verbrennen,“ und hieng sie nach der Reihe wieder hinauf.
sentences.l: Then he grew angry and said, “if you will not pay heed, then I cannot help you; I will not burn along with you,” and hung them up again in turn.
sentences.i: Then he grew angry and said, “If you won’t take care, I can’t help you — I’ve no mind to burn along with you,” and hung them up again one after the other.
sentences.n: ward — again the archaic preterite of werden (‘grew’). “Acht geben” = to pay attention, take care. hieng is the 1857 spelling of hing (preterite of hängen). “nach der Reihe” = in turn, in order.

sentences.t: Nun setzte er sich zu seinem Feuer und schlief ein, und am andern Morgen, da kam der Mann zu ihm, wollte die funfzig Thaler haben und sprach „nun, weißt du was gruseln ist?“
sentences.l: Now he sat down by his fire and fell asleep, and the next morning the man came to him, wanted the fifty thalers and said, “well, do you know what shuddering is?”
sentences.i: Now he sat down by his fire and fell asleep, and the next morning the man came to him, wanting his fifty thalers, and said, “Well, do you know now what shuddering is?”
sentences.n: “schlief ein” — einschlafen, to fall asleep (separable). “am andern Morgen” = the next morning (andern = the old spelling of anderen).

sentences.t: „Nein,“ antwortete er, „woher sollte ichs wissen? die da droben haben das Maul nicht aufgethan und waren so dumm, daß sie die paar alten Lappen, die sie am Leibe haben, brennen ließen.“
sentences.l: “No,” he answered, “how should I know it? those up there did not open their mouths and were so stupid that they let the few old rags they have on their bodies burn.”
sentences.i: “No,” he answered, “how should I know? Those fellows up there never opened their mouths, and were so stupid they let the few old rags on their backs burn.”
sentences.n: “woher sollte ichs wissen” = ‘how should I know it?’ (ichs = ich es). “das Maul aufthun” = to open one’s mouth, speak up (Maul is a coarse ‘mouth,’ properly an animal’s; aufgethan is the old spelling of aufgetan). Lappen = rags. “am Leibe” = on the body (with old dative -e).

sentences.t: Da sah der Mann daß er die funfzig Thaler heute nicht davon tragen würde, gieng fort und sprach „so einer ist mir noch nicht vorgekommen.“
sentences.l: Then the man saw that he would not carry off the fifty thalers today, went away and said, “such a one has not yet come my way.”
sentences.i: Then the man saw he would not be carrying off the fifty thalers that day; he went off, saying, “I never met the like of him.”
sentences.n: “davon tragen” = to carry off (davontragen). vorkommen (separable) = to occur, come one’s way; “so einer ist mir noch nicht vorgekommen” = ‘I’ve never come across such a one.’

sentences.t: Der Junge gieng auch seines Weges und fieng wieder an vor sich hin zu reden „ach, wenn mirs nur gruselte! ach, wenn mirs nur gruselte!“
sentences.l: The boy too went his way and began again to talk to himself, “oh, if only it shuddered me! oh, if only it shuddered me!”
sentences.i: The boy too went his way, and began once more to mutter to himself, “Oh, if only I could shudder! Oh, if only I could shudder!”
sentences.n: “seines Weges gehen” = to go on one’s way (genitive). fieng is the 1857 spelling of fing; “fieng ... an” — anfangen (separable), to begin. The refrain returns for the third setting.

sentences.t: Das hörte ein Fuhrmann, der hinter ihm her schritt, und fragte „wer bist du?“
sentences.l: That a carter heard, who strode along behind him, and asked, “who are you?”
sentences.i: A carter walking behind him heard it and asked, “Who are you?”
sentences.n: der Fuhrmann = a carter, a wagon-driver. “hinter ihm her” = along behind him. schritt = strode (preterite of schreiten).

sentences.t: „Ich weiß nicht“ antwortete der Junge.
sentences.l: “I do not know,” answered the boy.
sentences.i: “I don’t know,” answered the boy.

sentences.t: Der Fuhrmann fragte weiter „wo bist du her?“
sentences.l: The carter asked further, “where are you from?”
sentences.i: The carter went on, “Where are you from?”
sentences.n: “wo bist du her” = ‘where are you from’ (woher, split apart, as often in speech).

sentences.t: „Ich weiß nicht.“
sentences.l: “I do not know.”
sentences.i: “I don’t know.”

sentences.t: „Wer ist dein Vater?“
sentences.l: “Who is your father?”

sentences.t: „Das darf ich nicht sagen.“
sentences.l: “That I may not say.”
sentences.i: “That I’m not allowed to say.”
sentences.n: dürfen = to be allowed to; “das darf ich nicht sagen” = ‘I mustn’t say,’ obeying the father’s command back in the fifty-thaler speech.

sentences.t: „Was brummst du beständig in den Bart hinein?“
sentences.l: “What are you muttering constantly into your beard?”
sentences.i: “What are you forever mumbling into your beard?”
sentences.n: “in den Bart brummen” = to mutter into one’s beard, to mumble to oneself. beständig = constantly, continually.

sentences.t: „Ei,“ antwortete der Junge, „ich wollte, daß mirs gruselte, aber niemand kann mirs lehren.“
sentences.l: “Eh,” answered the boy, “I wished that it shuddered me, but no one can teach it to me.”
sentences.i: “Why,” answered the boy, “I wish I could shudder, but no one can teach me how.”
sentences.n: “ich wollte, daß ...” = ‘I wish that ...’ (wollte here is the subjunctive of a wish). “mirs lehren” — lehren with a double object, ‘to teach it to me’ (mirs = mir es).

sentences.t: „Laß dein dummes Geschwätz,“ sprach der Fuhrmann, „komm, geh mit mir, ich will sehen daß ich dich unterbringe.“
sentences.l: “Leave your stupid chatter,” said the carter, “come, go with me, I will see that I find you a place.”
sentences.i: “Give over your silly prattle,” said the carter; “come, go with me, and I’ll see about finding you a place.”
sentences.n: Geschwätz = idle chatter, prattle. “ich will sehen daß ...” = ‘I’ll see to it that ...’ unterbringen (separable) = to lodge, place, find room for.

sentences.t: Der Junge gieng mit dem Fuhrmann, und Abends gelangten sie zu einem Wirthshaus, wo sie übernachten wollten.
sentences.l: The boy went with the carter, and in the evening they arrived at an inn where they wanted to spend the night.
sentences.i: The boy went along with the carter, and toward evening they came to an inn where they meant to spend the night.
sentences.n: gelangen = to arrive, reach. Wirthshaus is the 1857 spelling of Wirtshaus (‘inn’), with the old -th-. übernachten = to stay the night.

sentences.t: Da sprach er beim Eintritt in die Stube wieder ganz laut „wenn mirs nur gruselte! wenn mirs nur gruselte!“
sentences.l: There, on entering the room, he again said quite loudly, “if only it shuddered me! if only it shuddered me!”
sentences.i: There, as he stepped into the parlour, he again said right out loud, “If only I could shudder! If only I could shudder!”
sentences.n: “beim Eintritt” = on entering (Eintritt = entry). die Stube = the (living-)room, parlour.

sentences.t: Der Wirth, der das hörte, lachte und sprach „wenn dich danach lüstet, dazu sollte hier wohl Gelegenheit sein.“
sentences.l: The innkeeper, who heard that, laughed and said, “if you have a craving for it, there should surely be opportunity for it here.”
sentences.i: The innkeeper, hearing it, laughed and said, “If that’s your fancy, there should be a chance for it hereabouts.”
sentences.n: der Wirth (1857 for Wirt) = the innkeeper, host. “wenn dich danach lüstet” — the impersonal lüsten with the accusative, ‘if you have a hankering for it’ (danach = ‘for it’). Gelegenheit = opportunity.

sentences.t: „Ach schweig stille,“ sprach die Wirthsfrau, „so mancher Vorwitzige hat schon sein Leben eingebüßt, es wäre Jammer und Schade um die schönen Augen, wenn die das Tageslicht nicht wieder sehen sollten.“
sentences.l: “Oh, hush,” said the landlady, “many a forward fellow has already forfeited his life; it would be a grief and a pity for those fine eyes, if they should never see the daylight again.”
sentences.i: “Oh, hush now,” said the landlady; “many a rash fellow has already lost his life over it; it would be a grief and a shame if those fine eyes should never see daylight again.”
sentences.n: “schweig stille” = ‘be quite silent, hush’ (schweigen). Wirthsfrau (1857) = the landlady. Vorwitzige = an over-curious, forward person (a nominalized adjective); “so mancher” = many a. “sein Leben einbüßen” = to lose one’s life (einbüßen, to forfeit). “Jammer und Schade” = a stock pairing, ‘grief and pity.’

sentences.t: Der Junge aber sagte „wenns noch so schwer wäre, ich wills einmal lernen, deshalb bin ich ja ausgezogen.“
sentences.l: But the boy said, “however hard it might be, I will learn it once and for all; that is just why I set out.”
sentences.i: But the boy said, “No matter how hard it may be, I mean to learn it; that’s the very reason I set out.”
sentences.n: “wenns noch so schwer wäre” = ‘however hard it might be’ (the concessive wenn ... noch so). “wills” = will es. “bin ... ausgezogen” — ausziehen (separable), to set out, go forth — the very verb of the title (der auszog das Fürchten zu lernen).

sentences.t: Er ließ dem Wirth auch keine Ruhe, bis dieser erzählte nicht weit davon stände ein verwünschtes Schloß, wo einer wohl lernen könnte was gruseln wäre, wenn er nur drei Nächte darin wachen wollte.
sentences.l: He gave the innkeeper no peace either, until the latter told that not far off there stood an enchanted castle, where one might well learn what shuddering was, if only he would keep watch three nights in it.
sentences.i: He gave the innkeeper no peace until the man told him that not far off stood an enchanted castle, where one might well learn what shuddering was, if only he would keep watch there three nights.
sentences.n: “keine Ruhe lassen” = to give (someone) no peace. verwünscht = enchanted, accursed, under a spell. The reported speech runs in the subjunctive — stände, könnte, wäre. wachen = to keep watch, stay awake. Nächte = nights (plural of Nacht).

sentences.t: Der König hätte dem, ders wagen wollte, seine Tochter zur Frau versprochen, und die wäre die schönste Jungfrau, welche die Sonne beschien: in dem Schlosse steckten auch große Schätze, von bösen Geistern bewacht, die würden dann frei und könnten einen Armen reich genug machen.
sentences.l: The king had promised his daughter as wife to whoever would dare it, and she was the fairest maiden the sun shone upon: in the castle there were also great treasures, guarded by evil spirits, which would then be freed and could make a poor man rich enough.
sentences.i: The king had promised his daughter in marriage to anyone who would dare it, and she was the fairest maiden the sun ever shone upon; in the castle, too, lay great treasures guarded by evil spirits, which would then be set free and could make a poor man rich enough.
sentences.n: Still the innkeeper’s report, hence the string of subjunctives hätte, wäre, würden, könnten. “dem, ders wagen wollte” = ‘to him who would dare it’ (ders = der es). beschien = shone upon (preterite of bescheinen). bewacht = guarded (bewachen). Geistern = spirits (dative plural of Geist).

sentences.t: Schon viele wären wohl hinein aber noch keiner wieder heraus gekommen.
sentences.l: Many had indeed gone in, but as yet none had come out again.
sentences.i: Many, indeed, had gone in, but not one had ever come out again.
sentences.n: Reported speech again (wären). “hinein ... gekommen” and “heraus gekommen” — hineinkommen and herauskommen, in and out relative to the castle.

sentences.t: Da gieng der Junge am andern Morgen vor den König und sprach „wenns erlaubt wäre, so wollte ich wohl drei Nächte in dem verwünschten Schlosse wachen.“
sentences.l: So the next morning the boy went before the king and said, “if it were permitted, I would gladly keep watch three nights in the enchanted castle.”
sentences.i: So the next morning the boy went before the king and said, “If it be permitted, I should gladly keep watch three nights in the enchanted castle.”
sentences.n: “vor den König gehen” = to go before, into the presence of, the king. “wenns erlaubt wäre ... so wollte ich” — a polite conditional (‘if it were allowed, I would’).

sentences.t: Der König sah ihn an, und weil er ihm gefiel, sprach er „du darfst dir noch dreierlei ausbitten, aber es müssen leblose Dinge sein, und darfst das du mit ins Schloß nehmen.“
sentences.l: The king looked at him, and because he pleased him, said, “you may request three things besides, but they must be lifeless things, and you may take them with you into the castle.”
sentences.i: The king looked at him, and because he took a liking to him, said, “You may ask for three things more, but they must be lifeless things, and you may take them into the castle with you.”
sentences.n: “sah ihn an” — ansehen (separable). “er gefiel ihm” = ‘he pleased him’ (gefallen + dative). “sich ausbitten” = to request, ask for (for oneself). dreierlei = three (different) kinds of things. leblos = lifeless, inanimate. (The clause “darfst das du mit ... nehmen” is loosely built — read: ‘and you may take them with you.’)

sentences.t: Da antwortete er „so bitt ich um ein Feuer, eine Drehbank und eine Schnitzbank mit dem Messer.“
sentences.l: Then he answered, “then I ask for a fire, a lathe and a carving-bench with the knife.”
sentences.i: Then he answered, “Then I ask for a fire, a lathe, and a carving-bench with its knife.”
sentences.n: “bitten um” = to ask for. Drehbank = a lathe (a ‘turning-bench’); Schnitzbank = a carving-bench or shaving-horse. These three ‘lifeless things’ become his tools against the castle’s terrors.

sentences.t: Der König ließ ihm das alles bei Tage in das Schloß tragen.
sentences.l: The king had all this carried into the castle for him by day.
sentences.i: The king had it all carried into the castle for him by daylight.
sentences.n: “ließ ... tragen” = had (it) carried (lassen + infinitive, the causative). “bei Tage” = by day (with the old dative -e).

sentences.t: Als es Nacht werden wollte, gieng der Junge hinauf, machte sich in einer Kammer ein helles Feuer an, stellte die Schnitzbank mit dem Messer daneben und setzte sich auf die Drehbank.
sentences.l: When it was about to become night, the boy went up, made himself a bright fire in a chamber, set the carving-bench with the knife beside it and sat down on the lathe.
sentences.i: When night was coming on, the boy went up, lit himself a bright fire in a chamber, set the carving-bench with its knife beside it, and sat down on the lathe.
sentences.n: “als es Nacht werden wollte” = ‘when night was about to fall’ (wollen shading toward ‘be on the point of’). Kammer = a small room, a chamber. “machte ... an” — anmachen, to light. daneben = beside it.

sentences.t: „Ach, wenn mirs nur gruselte!“ sprach er, „aber hier werde ichs auch nicht lernen.“
sentences.l: “Oh, if only it shuddered me!” he said, “but here too I shall not learn it.”
sentences.i: “Oh, if only I could shudder!” he said; “but here too I shan’t learn it.”

sentences.t: Gegen Mitternacht wollte er sich sein Feuer einmal aufschüren: wie er so hinein blies, da schries plötzlich aus einer Ecke „au, miau! was uns friert!“
sentences.l: Toward midnight he wanted to stir up his fire a bit: as he was blowing into it, suddenly it cried out from a corner, “ow, miaow! how cold we are!”
sentences.i: Toward midnight he thought to poke up his fire; and as he blew into it, a voice suddenly screeched from a corner, “Ow, miaow! how cold we are!”
sentences.n: aufschüren = to stir/poke up (a fire). “schries” = schrie es (‘it cried out’), with the contracted es. “was uns friert” — the impersonal frieren, literally ‘how it freezes us,’ i.e. ‘how cold we are.’ au and miau are the interjections.

sentences.t: „Ihr Narren,“ rief er, „was schreit ihr? wenn euch friert, kommt, setzt euch ans Feuer und wärmt euch.“
sentences.l: “You fools,” he cried, “what are you screaming for? if you are cold, come, sit down by the fire and warm yourselves.”
sentences.i: “You fools,” he cried, “what are you screeching for? If you’re cold, come and sit by the fire and warm yourselves.”
sentences.n: Narren = fools (singular Narr). “was schreit ihr” = ‘why are you screaming’ (schreien). “wenn euch friert” — impersonal frieren, ‘if you’re cold.’ “wärmt euch” = warm yourselves (reflexive).

sentences.t: Und wie er das gesagt hatte, kamen zwei große schwarze Katzen in einem gewaltigen Sprunge herbei, setzten sich ihm zu beiden Seiten und sahen ihn mit ihren feurigen Augen ganz wild an.
sentences.l: And when he had said that, two great black cats came up in a mighty leap, set themselves on both sides of him and looked at him quite wildly with their fiery eyes.
sentences.i: And when he had said that, two great black cats came bounding up in one tremendous leap, seated themselves on either side of him, and glared at him fiercely with their fiery eyes.
sentences.n: Katzen = cats. “in einem gewaltigen Sprunge” = in one mighty bound (Sprung). “sahen ... an” — ansehen, to look at; wild = fiercely, wildly.

sentences.t: Über ein Weilchen, als sie sich gewärmt hatten, sprachen sie „Kamerad, wollen wir eins in der Karte spielen?“
sentences.l: After a little while, when they had warmed themselves, they said, “comrade, shall we play a hand of cards?”
sentences.i: After a little while, when they had warmed themselves, they said, “Comrade, shall we have a game of cards?”
sentences.n: “über ein Weilchen” = after a little while. “sich wärmen” = to warm oneself (gewärmt = warmed). “eins in der Karte spielen” = to play a round of cards (an old idiom; Karte = the cards).

sentences.t: „warum nicht?“ antwortete er, „aber zeigt einmal eure Pfoten her.“
sentences.l: “why not?” he answered, “but just show your paws here.”
sentences.i: “Why not?” he answered; “but first show me your paws.”
sentences.n: “zeigt ... her” — herzeigen, to show (toward the speaker); zeigt is a plural imperative. Pfoten = paws — he already sees they are animals, not gamblers.

sentences.t: Da streckten sie die Krallen aus.
sentences.l: Then they stretched out their claws.
sentences.i: Then they put out their claws.
sentences.n: “streckten ... aus” — ausstrecken, to stretch/put out (separable). Krallen = claws.

sentences.t: „Ei,“ sagte er, „was habt ihr lange Nägel! wartet, die muß ich euch erst abschneiden.“
sentences.l: “Eh,” he said, “what long nails you have! wait, I must first cut them off for you.”
sentences.i: “Why,” he said, “what long nails you have! Wait — I must trim them for you first.”
sentences.n: Nägel = nails, claws (singular Nagel). “die muß ich euch ... abschneiden” — abschneiden (separable), to cut off, with the dative euch (‘for you’). wartet = plural imperative.

sentences.t: Damit packte er sie beim Kragen, hob sie auf die Schnitzbank und schraubte ihnen die Pfoten fest.
sentences.l: With that he grabbed them by the collar, lifted them onto the carving-bench and screwed their paws fast.
sentences.i: With that he seized them by the scruff, lifted them onto the carving-bench, and screwed their paws down tight.
sentences.n: “beim Kragen packen” = to grab by the collar, the scruff. “schraubte ... fest” — festschrauben, to screw fast/tight (separable), with the dative ihnen (‘their paws’). The carving-bench’s clamp becomes a trap.

sentences.t: „Euch habe ich auf die Finger gesehen,“ sprach er, „da vergeht mir die Lust zum Kartenspiel,“ schlug sie todt und warf sie hinaus ins Wasser.
sentences.l: “I have watched your fingers,” he said, “now my appetite for the card-game passes,” struck them dead and threw them out into the water.
sentences.i: “I’ve had my eye on you,” he said; “now I’ve lost all taste for cards,” and struck them dead and threw them out into the water.
sentences.n: “jemandem auf die Finger sehen” is an idiom, ‘to watch someone closely, keep an eye on their tricks.’ “da vergeht mir die Lust” = ‘my desire for it passes’ (vergehen + dative). todt is the 1857 spelling of tot (‘dead’); “schlug sie todt” = struck them dead. Kartenspiel = card-game.

sentences.t: Als er aber die zwei zur Ruhe gebracht hatte und sich wieder zu seinem Feuer setzen wollte, da kamen aus allen Ecken und Enden schwarze Katzen und schwarze Hunde an glühenden Ketten, immer mehr und mehr, daß er sich nicht mehr bergen konnte: die schrien gräulich, traten ihm auf sein Feuer, zerrten es auseinander und wollten es ausmachen.
sentences.l: But when he had brought the two to rest and wanted to sit down again by his fire, there came from all corners and ends black cats and black dogs on glowing chains, ever more and more, so that he could no longer shelter himself: they screamed hideously, trod on his fire, dragged it apart and wanted to put it out.
sentences.i: But when he had put the two to rest and was about to sit down by his fire again, from every corner and cranny came black cats and black dogs on glowing chains, more and more of them, till he could find no room to shelter; they shrieked hideously, trampled his fire, dragged it apart, and tried to put it out.
sentences.n: “zur Ruhe bringen” = to put to rest (here, to death). “aus allen Ecken und Enden” = from every nook and cranny (Enden = ends, corners). glühend = glowing, red-hot; Ketten = chains. “sich bergen” = to shelter, take cover. gräulich = horribly, gruesomely. “traten ... auf” — treten auf, to tread on. “zerrten ... auseinander” — auseinanderzerren, to drag apart; ausmachen = to put out (a fire).

sentences.t: Das sah er ein Weilchen ruhig mit an, als es ihm aber zu arg ward, faßte er sein Schnitzmesser und rief „fort mit dir, du Gesindel,“ und haute auf sie los.
sentences.l: That he watched calmly for a little while, but when it grew too bad for him, he seized his carving-knife and cried, “away with you, you rabble,” and laid into them.
sentences.i: He watched it calmly for a little while, but when it grew too much for him, he seized his carving-knife and cried, “Off with you, you rabble!” and laid about him at them.
sentences.n: “sah ... mit an” — mit ansehen, to look on at. “zu arg werden” = to get too bad (arg = grievous). Schnitzmesser = the carving-knife (his tool). Gesindel = rabble, riff-raff. “haute auf sie los” — loshauen, to lay into, strike out at (haute is the preterite of hauen).

sentences.t: Ein Theil sprang weg, die andern schlug er todt und warf sie hinaus in den Teich.
sentences.l: A part sprang away, the others he struck dead and threw out into the pond.
sentences.i: Some sprang away; the rest he struck dead and threw out into the pond.
sentences.n: Theil is the 1857 spelling of Teil (‘part’); “ein Theil” = some of them. Teich = pond. todt again = dead.

sentences.t: Als er wieder gekommen war, blies er aus den Funken sein Feuer frisch an und wärmte sich.
sentences.l: When he had come back, he blew his fire fresh from the sparks and warmed himself.
sentences.i: When he had come back, he blew his fire up afresh from the embers and warmed himself.
sentences.n: “blies ... an” — anblasen, to blow (a fire) alight. Funken = sparks, embers (from Funke). frisch here = anew, afresh.

sentences.t: Und als er so saß, wollten ihm die Augen nicht länger offen bleiben und er bekam Lust zu schlafen.
sentences.l: And as he sat thus, his eyes would no longer stay open and he got a desire to sleep.
sentences.i: And as he sat there, his eyes would no longer stay open, and he grew drowsy.
sentences.n: “wollten ihm die Augen nicht ... bleiben” — wollen here of things ‘refusing’ to (his eyes would not stay open). “Lust bekommen” = to get an urge, to feel inclined.

sentences.t: Da blickte er um sich und sah in der Ecke ein großes Bett, „das ist mir eben recht“ sprach er und legte sich hinein.
sentences.l: Then he looked about him and saw a great bed in the corner, “that suits me just right,” he said and lay down in it.
sentences.i: Then he looked about him and saw a great bed in the corner. “That’s just the thing for me,” he said, and lay down in it.
sentences.n: “um sich blicken” = to look around. “das ist mir eben recht” = ‘that suits me nicely’ (eben = ‘just,’ recht = ‘right, welcome’). “legte sich hinein” — sich hineinlegen, to lie down into it.

sentences.t: Als er aber die Augen zuthun wollte, so fieng das Bett von selbst an zu fahren, und fuhr im ganzen Schloß herum.
sentences.l: But when he wanted to close his eyes, the bed began of itself to move, and drove round through the whole castle.
sentences.i: But just as he was about to shut his eyes, the bed began to move of its own accord, and went careering all through the castle.
sentences.n: “die Augen zuthun” = to close one’s eyes (zuthun, the 1857 spelling of zutun, separable). “von selbst” = of its own accord. “fieng ... an” — anfangen (1857 fieng). fahren here = to travel, move, go (of the runaway bed).

sentences.t: „Recht so,“ sprach er, „nur besser zu.“
sentences.l: “Right so,” he said, “only faster on.”
sentences.i: “That’s the way,” he said, “faster still!”
sentences.n: “recht so” = ‘that’s right, well done.’ “nur besser zu” = ‘just go to it harder, faster’ — the boy cheerfully eggs the runaway bed on.

sentences.t: Da rollte das Bett fort, als wären sechs Pferde vorgespannt, über Schwellen und Treppen auf und ab: auf einmal hopp hopp! warf es um, das unterste zu oberst, daß es wie ein Berg auf ihm lag.
sentences.l: Then the bed rolled on, as if six horses were harnessed to it, over thresholds and stairs, up and down: all at once, hop hop! it overturned, the bottom uppermost, so that it lay on him like a mountain.
sentences.i: Then the bed rolled on as if six horses were harnessed to it, over thresholds and stairs, up and down; then all at once — hop, hop! — it turned over, bottom over top, so that it lay on him like a mountain.
sentences.n: “als wären sechs Pferde vorgespannt” = as if six horses were harnessed in front (subjunctive of comparison; vorspannen). Schwellen = thresholds, door-sills. “das unterste zu oberst” = ‘the bottom uppermost,’ i.e. upside down (oberst and unterst are superlatives). hopp is the bouncing interjection.

sentences.t: Aber er schleuderte Decken und Kissen in die Höhe, stieg heraus und sagte „nun mag fahren wer Lust hat,“ legte sich an sein Feuer und schlief bis es Tag war.
sentences.l: But he hurled blankets and pillows into the air, climbed out and said, “now let whoever likes go for a ride,” lay down by his fire and slept until it was day.
sentences.i: But he flung the blankets and pillows into the air, climbed out, and said, “Now let whoever pleases go careering about,” and lay down by his fire and slept till daybreak.
sentences.n: schleudern = to hurl, fling. Decken = blankets, covers (Decke). “nun mag fahren wer Lust hat” = ‘now let anyone who feels like it ride’ (mögen expressing ‘let, may’). His unshakable calm is the running joke.

sentences.t: Am Morgen kam der König, und als er ihn da auf der Erde liegen sah, meinte er die Gespenster hätten ihn umgebracht, und er wäre todt.
sentences.l: In the morning the king came, and when he saw him lying there on the ground, he supposed the ghosts had killed him and that he was dead.
sentences.i: In the morning the king came, and when he saw him lying there on the ground, he thought the ghosts had done him in and that he was dead.
sentences.n: Gespenster = ghosts, spectres. The reported thought stands in the subjunctive: hätten ... umgebracht, wäre. umbringen = to kill, do away with. todt = dead (1857).

sentences.t: Da sprach er „es ist doch schade um den schönen Menschen.“
sentences.l: Then he said, “it is a pity, though, about the handsome young man.”
sentences.i: Then he said, “What a pity, though, about the handsome young fellow.”
sentences.n: “es ist schade um ...” = ‘it’s a pity about ...’ (Schade = pity, loss). doch here = ‘after all, though.’

sentences.t: Das hörte der Junge, richtete sich auf und sprach „so weit ists noch nicht!“
sentences.l: The boy heard that, raised himself up and said, “it has not come to that yet!”
sentences.i: The boy heard it, sat up, and said, “It hasn’t come to that yet!”
sentences.n: “richtete sich auf” — sich aufrichten, to sit or raise oneself up. “so weit ists noch nicht” = ‘it hasn’t come to that yet’ (ists = ist es).

sentences.t: Da verwunderte sich der König, freute sich aber, und fragte wie es ihm gegangen wäre.
sentences.l: Then the king was astonished, but was glad, and asked how it had gone for him.
sentences.i: Then the king was astonished, but glad, and asked how he had fared.
sentences.n: “sich verwundern” = to be astonished; “sich freuen” = to be glad. “wie es ihm gegangen wäre” = how he had fared (the impersonal es geht jemandem; subjunctive of the reported question).

sentences.t: „Recht gut,“ antwortete er, „eine Nacht wäre herum, die zwei andern werden auch herum gehen.“
sentences.l: “Quite well,” he answered, “one night is over, the two others will pass too.”
sentences.i: “Quite well,” he answered; “one night’s got by, and the other two will pass as well.”
sentences.n: “herum sein” = to be over, past; “herum gehen” = to pass, go by (of time). wäre is a light subjunctive here.

sentences.t: Als er zum Wirth kam, da machte der große Augen.
sentences.l: When he came to the innkeeper, the latter made big eyes.
sentences.i: When he came to the innkeeper, the man’s eyes went wide.
sentences.n: “große Augen machen” is an idiom, ‘to be wide-eyed with astonishment.’ “der” here = ‘the latter, that one’ (the innkeeper).

sentences.t: „Ich dachte nicht,“ sprach er, „daß ich dich wieder lebendig sehen würde; hast du nun gelernt was Gruseln ist?“
sentences.l: “I did not think,” he said, “that I would see you alive again; have you now learned what shuddering is?”
sentences.i: “I never thought,” he said, “to see you alive again; have you learned now what shuddering is?”
sentences.n: lebendig = alive. “hast du gelernt” — the perfect of lernen. The innkeeper asks the tale’s standing question.

sentences.t: „Nein,“ sagte er, „es ist alles vergeblich: wenn mirs nur einer sagen könnte!“
sentences.l: “No,” he said, “it is all in vain: if only someone could tell me!”
sentences.i: “No,” he said, “it’s all in vain — if only someone could tell me what it is!”
sentences.n: vergeblich = in vain. “wenn mirs nur einer sagen könnte” = ‘if only someone could tell me’ (mirs = mir es; einer = ‘someone’).

sentences.t: Die zweite Nacht gieng er abermals hinauf ins alte Schloß, setzte sich zum Feuer und fieng sein altes Lied wieder an, „wenn mirs nur gruselte!“
sentences.l: The second night he went up again into the old castle, sat down by the fire and struck up his old song again, “if only it shuddered me!”
sentences.i: The second night he went up again into the old castle, sat down by the fire, and struck up his old refrain, “If only I could shudder!”
sentences.n: abermals = once again. “sein altes Lied anfangen” = to strike up one’s old song, i.e. to fall back into the same old refrain (fieng ... an, anfangen).

sentences.t: Wie Mitternacht herankam, ließ sich ein Lärm und Gepolter hören, erst sachte, dann immer stärker, dann wars ein bischen still, endlich kam mit lautem Geschrei ein halber Mensch den Schornstein herab und fiel vor ihn hin.
sentences.l: As midnight drew near, a noise and rumbling made itself heard, first softly, then ever louder, then it was a little still, at last, with loud shrieking, half a man came down the chimney and fell down before him.
sentences.i: As midnight drew near, a noise and a clattering was heard, softly at first, then louder and louder, then for a moment still; at last, with a loud shriek, half a man came down the chimney and fell down in front of him.
sentences.n: “sich hören lassen” = to make itself heard. Lärm = noise; Gepolter = a rumbling, clattering din. sachte = softly, gently. stärker = louder, stronger. bischen (1857 for bisschen) = a bit. Schornstein = chimney. “fiel ... hin” — hinfallen, to fall down.

sentences.t: „Heda!“ rief er, „noch ein halber gehört dazu, das ist zu wenig.“
sentences.l: “Hey there!” he cried, “another half belongs to it, that is too little.”
sentences.i: “Hey there!” he cried, “there’s another half wanted — that’s too little.”
sentences.n: Heda = ‘hey there!’ “ein halber” = a half one (halb used as a noun). “gehört dazu” = belongs to it, is wanted (dazugehören). The boy’s coolness before a half-corpse is the black comedy.

sentences.t: Da gieng der Lärm von frischem an, es tobte und heulte, und fiel die andere Hälfte auch herab.
sentences.l: Then the noise started up afresh, there was raging and howling, and the other half fell down too.
sentences.i: Then the noise started up afresh, with raging and howling, and the other half came down as well.
sentences.n: “von frischem” = anew, afresh. “gieng ... an” — angehen, to start up. toben = to rage, storm; heulen = to howl. Hälfte = a half.

sentences.t: „Wart,“ sprach er, „ich will dir erst das Feuer ein wenig anblasen.“
sentences.l: “Wait,” he said, “I will first blow up the fire for you a little.”
sentences.i: “Wait,” he said, “I’ll blow up the fire a little for you first.”
sentences.n: anblasen = to blow on/up (a fire). The boy addresses the reassembling corpse as a chilly guest, with the dative dir (‘for you’).

sentences.t: Wie er das gethan hatte und sich wieder umsah, da waren die beiden Stücke zusammen gefahren, und saß da ein gräulicher Mann auf seinem Platz.
sentences.l: When he had done that and looked around again, the two pieces had come together, and there sat a hideous man in his place.
sentences.i: When he had done that and looked round again, the two halves had rushed together, and there sat a hideous man in his seat.
sentences.n: gethan = done (1857 for getan). “sich umsehen” = to look around (umsah, preterite). Stücke = pieces. “zusammen gefahren” — zusammenfahren, here ‘to rush, come together.’ gräulich = hideous, gruesome; “auf seinem Platz” = in his (the boy’s) seat.

sentences.t: „So haben wir nicht gewettet,“ sprach der Junge, „die Bank ist mein.“
sentences.l: “That is not what we wagered,” said the boy, “the bench is mine.”
sentences.i: “That wasn’t the bargain,” said the boy; “the bench is mine.”
sentences.n: “so haben wir nicht gewettet” is a set idiom, literally ‘that’s not what we bet,’ i.e. ‘that wasn’t the deal.’ Bank = the bench (his seat).

sentences.t: Der Mann wollte ihn wegdrängen, aber der Junge ließ sichs nicht gefallen, schob ihn mit Gewalt weg und setzte sich wieder auf seinen Platz.
sentences.l: The man wanted to push him away, but the boy did not put up with it, shoved him off by force and sat down again in his place.
sentences.i: The man tried to shove him aside, but the boy would not stand for it, pushed him off by force, and sat down again in his place.
sentences.n: wegdrängen = to push away, crowd out. “sich etwas gefallen lassen” = to put up with, stand for something (“ließ sichs nicht gefallen” = would not stand for it). “schob ... weg” — wegschieben; mit Gewalt = by force.

sentences.t: Da fielen noch mehr Männer herab, einer nach dem andern, die holten neun Todtenbeine und zwei Todtenköpfe, setzten auf und spielten Kegel.
sentences.l: Then still more men fell down, one after the other; they fetched nine dead men’s bones and two skulls, set them up and played ninepins.
sentences.i: Then more men came tumbling down, one after another; they fetched nine dead men’s bones and two skulls, set them up, and played at ninepins.
sentences.n: This is the tale’s famous scene. Todtenbeine = dead men’s bones (Todtenbein; 1857 -dt-), used as the ninepins; Todtenköpfe = skulls (Totenkopf), used as the balls. Kegel = ninepins, skittles; “Kegel spielen” = to bowl. “setzten auf” — aufsetzen, to set up (the pins).

sentences.t: Der Junge bekam auch Lust und fragte „hört ihr, kann ich mit sein?“
sentences.l: The boy too got a desire and asked, “listen, can I be in on it?”
sentences.i: The boy too felt like joining, and asked, “I say, can I be in it?”
sentences.n: “Lust bekommen” = to feel like it, take a fancy. “kann ich mit sein” = ‘may I join in?’ (mit = ‘along, in on it’).

sentences.t: „Ja, wenn du Geld hast.“
sentences.l: “Yes, if you have money.”

sentences.t: „Geld genug,“ antwortete er, „aber eure Kugeln sind nicht recht rund.“
sentences.l: “Money enough,” he answered, “but your balls are not quite round.”
sentences.i: “Money enough,” he answered, “but your balls aren’t properly round.”
sentences.n: Kugeln = balls (here the skulls used as bowling-balls). “nicht recht rund” = not quite round — the boy’s matter-of-fact complaint about skulls is the joke.

sentences.t: Da nahm er die Todtenköpfe, setzte sie in die Drehbank und drehte sie rund.
sentences.l: Then he took the skulls, set them in the lathe and turned them round.
sentences.i: Then he took the skulls, put them in the lathe, and turned them round.
sentences.n: Todtenköpfe = skulls. drehen = to turn (on a lathe, Drehbank) — this is why he asked for the lathe.

sentences.t: „So, jetzt werden sie besser schüppeln,“ sprach er, „heida! nun gehts lustig!“
sentences.l: “There, now they will roll better,” he said, “hey-ho! now it goes merrily!”
sentences.i: “There, now they’ll roll better,” he said; “hey-ho! now the fun begins!”
sentences.n: schüppeln is a dialect word for the balls rolling, trundling. heida is a merry interjection (‘hey-ho, whee’). “nun gehts lustig” = ‘now it goes merrily’ (gehts = geht es).

sentences.t: Er spielte mit und verlor etwas von seinem Geld, als es aber zwölf Uhr schlug, war alles vor seinen Augen verschwunden.
sentences.l: He played along and lost some of his money, but when it struck twelve o’clock, everything had vanished before his eyes.
sentences.i: He joined in and lost a little of his money, but when the clock struck twelve, everything had vanished before his eyes.
sentences.n: “spielte mit” — mitspielen, to join in the game. “zwölf Uhr schlug” = struck twelve (Uhr = o’clock). Midnight’s end dispels the spectres, as with the cats and the bed.

sentences.t: Er legte sich nieder und schlief ruhig ein.
sentences.l: He lay down and fell quietly asleep.
sentences.i: He lay down and fell peacefully asleep.
sentences.n: “legte sich nieder” — sich niederlegen, to lie down. “schlief ... ein” — einschlafen, to fall asleep.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen kam der König und wollte sich erkundigen.
sentences.l: The next morning the king came and wanted to inquire.
sentences.i: The next morning the king came, wishing to inquire.
sentences.n: “sich erkundigen” = to inquire, ask after (how things had gone).

sentences.t: „Wie ist dirs diesmal gegangen?“ fragte er.
sentences.l: “How has it gone for you this time?” he asked.
sentences.i: “How did you fare this time?” he asked.
sentences.n: “wie ist dirs gegangen” = ‘how did you get on?’ (dirs = dir es; the impersonal es geht jemandem).

sentences.t: „Ich habe gekegelt,“ antwortete er, „und ein paar Heller verloren.“
sentences.l: “I have bowled,” he answered, “and lost a couple of hellers.”
sentences.i: “I’ve been bowling,” he answered, “and lost a couple of hellers.”
sentences.n: kegeln = to bowl, play ninepins (gekegelt). der Heller was a heller, a small old copper coin (a trifling sum).

sentences.t: „Hat dir denn nicht gegruselt?“
sentences.l: “Did it then not shudder you?”
sentences.i: “Did you not get the creeps, then?”
sentences.n: “hat dir nicht gegruselt” — the impersonal gruseln in the perfect, with the dative dir (‘did it not give you the creeps?’). gegruselt is its past participle. denn = ‘then’ (a probing particle).

sentences.t: „Ei was,“ sprach er, „lustig hab ich mich gemacht.
sentences.l: “Eh, what,” he said, “I made merry.
sentences.i: “Pooh,” he said, “I had a merry time of it.
sentences.n: “ei was” = a dismissive ‘pooh, nonsense.’ “sich lustig machen” here = to make merry, enjoy oneself (rather than the modern ‘to make fun of’).

sentences.t: Wenn ich nur wüßte was Gruseln wäre?“
sentences.l: If only I knew what shuddering is?”
sentences.i: If only I knew what shuddering is!”
sentences.n: “wenn ich nur wüßte” = ‘if only I knew’ (wüßte, the subjunctive of wissen; 1857 with ß). The boy is still baffled.

sentences.t: In der dritten Nacht setzte er sich wieder auf seine Bank und sprach ganz verdrießlich „wenn es mir nur gruselte!“
sentences.l: On the third night he sat down again on his bench and said quite peevishly, “if only it shuddered me!”
sentences.i: On the third night he sat down again on his bench and said quite sullenly, “If only I could shudder!”
sentences.n: verdrießlich = sullen, peevish, out of sorts. Note the fuller “wenn es mir nur gruselte” (elsewhere contracted to mirs) — the same impersonal gruseln.

sentences.t: Als es spät ward kamen sechs große Männer und brachten eine Todtenlade hereingetragen.
sentences.l: When it grew late, six big men came and brought a coffin carried in.
sentences.i: When it grew late, six big men came, carrying in a coffin.
sentences.n: ward — the archaic preterite of werden. Todtenlade = a coffin, a ‘death-chest’ (1857 -dt-; Lade = chest). “brachten ... hereingetragen” — a doubling of bringen with the participle of hereintragen (‘carried in’), a folk-narrative flourish.

sentences.t: Da sprach er „ha ha, das ist gewiß mein Vetterchen, das erst vor ein paar Tagen gestorben ist,“ winkte mit dem Finger und rief „komm, Vetterchen, komm!“
sentences.l: Then he said, “ha ha, that is surely my little cousin, who died only a couple of days ago,” beckoned with his finger and called, “come, little cousin, come!”
sentences.i: Then he said, “Ha ha, that must be my dear little cousin, who died only a few days ago,” beckoned with his finger, and called, “Come, little cousin, come!”
sentences.n: gewiß (1857 for gewiss) = certainly, surely. Vetter = a (male) cousin; Vetterchen is its affectionate diminutive, ‘dear little cousin.’ winken = to beckon, wave. The boy’s mock-welcome to the coffin is the grim joke.

sentences.t: Sie stellten den Sarg auf die Erde, er aber gieng hinzu und nahm den Deckel ab: da lag ein todter Mann darin.
sentences.l: They set the coffin on the ground, but he went up to it and took off the lid: there lay a dead man in it.
sentences.i: They set the coffin on the ground, but he went up to it and took off the lid; there lay a dead man inside.
sentences.n: der Sarg = coffin. hinzu = up to it, toward it. “nahm ... ab” — abnehmen, to take off (the Deckel, lid). todter = dead (1857 todt).

sentences.t: Er fühlte ihm ans Gesicht, aber es war kalt wie Eis.
sentences.l: He felt to his face, but it was cold as ice.
sentences.i: He felt his face, but it was cold as ice.
sentences.n: “fühlte ihm ans Gesicht” — the dative ihm (‘to him’) with the preposition, ‘felt his face’ (German prefers a dative to a possessive). “kalt wie Eis” = cold as ice.

sentences.t: „Wart,“ sprach er, „ich will dich ein bischen wärmen,“ gieng ans Feuer, wärmte seine Hand und legte sie ihm aufs Gesicht, aber der Todte blieb kalt.
sentences.l: “Wait,” he said, “I will warm you a little,” went to the fire, warmed his hand and laid it on his face, but the dead man stayed cold.
sentences.i: “Wait,” he said, “I’ll warm you a little,” went to the fire, warmed his hand, and laid it on the man’s face — but the dead man stayed cold.
sentences.n: bischen (1857) = a little. wärmen = to warm. der Todte = the dead man (a nominalized adjective). His tender attempt to warm a corpse is pure black comedy.

sentences.t: Nun nahm er ihn heraus, setzte sich ans Feuer und legte ihn auf seinen Schooß, und rieb ihm die Arme, damit das Blut wieder in Bewegung kommen sollte.
sentences.l: Now he took him out, sat down by the fire and laid him on his lap, and rubbed his arms, so that the blood should come into motion again.
sentences.i: Now he took him out, sat down by the fire, laid him on his lap, and rubbed his arms, so that the blood might start moving again.
sentences.n: “nahm ... heraus” — herausnehmen. Schooß is the 1857 spelling of Schoß (‘lap’). reiben = to rub (rieb). “in Bewegung kommen” = to start moving (Bewegung = motion). The dative ihm = ‘his arms.’

sentences.t: Als auch das nichts helfen wollte, fiel ihm ein „wenn zwei zusammen im Bett liegen, so wärmen sie sich,“ brachte ihn ins Bett, deckte ihn zu und legte sich neben ihn.
sentences.l: When even that would not help, it occurred to him, “when two lie together in bed, they warm each other,” brought him into the bed, covered him up and lay down beside him.
