sentences.i: When even that did no good, it came to him: “When two lie together in bed, they keep each other warm,” and he carried him to the bed, covered him up, and lay down beside him.
sentences.n: “es fällt jemandem ein” = it occurs to one (einfallen, separable; “fiel ihm ein”). “deckte ... zu” — zudecken, to cover up. His homely logic, applied to a corpse, sets up the scare that follows.

sentences.t: Über ein Weilchen ward auch der Todte warm und fieng an sich zu regen.
sentences.l: After a little while the dead man too grew warm and began to stir.
sentences.i: After a little while the dead man grew warm too, and began to stir.
sentences.n: ward — archaic ‘grew.’ “fieng an” — anfangen (1857). “sich regen” = to stir, move. Now the corpse revives — but not with gratitude.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Junge „siehst du, Vetterchen, hätt ich dich nicht gewärmt!“
sentences.l: Then the boy said, “you see, little cousin, had I not warmed you!”
sentences.i: Then the boy said, “You see, little cousin — suppose I hadn’t warmed you!”
sentences.n: “hätt ich dich nicht gewärmt” = ‘had I not warmed you’ (subjunctive II of the unreal condition, hätte contracted to hätt). gewärmt = warmed.

sentences.t: Der Todte aber hub an und rief „jetzt will ich dich erwürgen.“
sentences.l: The dead man, however, began and cried, “now I will strangle you.”
sentences.i: But the dead man began and cried, “Now I’ll strangle you!”
sentences.n: hub is the archaic preterite of heben in the phrase anheben (‘to begin, start up’; modern hob an). erwürgen = to strangle, throttle. The corpse’s ingratitude is the twist.

sentences.t: „Was,“ sagte er, „ist das mein Dank? gleich sollst du wieder in deinen Sarg,“ hub ihn auf, warf ihn hinein und machte den Deckel zu; da kamen die sechs Männer, und trugen ihn wieder fort.
sentences.l: “What,” he said, “is that my thanks? at once you shall go back into your coffin,” lifted him up, threw him in and shut the lid; then the six men came and carried him away again.
sentences.i: “What,” he said, “is that my thanks? Back into your coffin with you this instant!” — lifted him up, threw him in, and shut the lid; then the six men came and carried him off again.
sentences.n: “ist das mein Dank?” = ‘is that my thanks?’ gleich = at once. “sollst du wieder in deinen Sarg” — the verb of motion is dropped after sollst (‘you shall [go] back into your coffin’). “hub ... auf” — aufheben (archaic hub for hob), to lift up. “machte ... zu” — zumachen, to shut.

sentences.t: „Es will mir nicht gruseln,“ sagte er, „hier lerne ichs mein Lebtag nicht.“
sentences.l: “It will not shudder me,” he said, “here I shall never learn it in my life.”
sentences.i: “I just can’t seem to shudder,” he said; “I’ll never learn it here as long as I live.”
sentences.n: “es will mir nicht gruseln” = ‘the shuddering just won’t come to me’ (impersonal gruseln with dative mir; wollen of a thing ‘refusing’). “mein Lebtag nicht” = ‘never in my life’ (Lebtag = livelong day).

sentences.t: Da trat ein Mann herein, der war größer als alle andere, und sah fürchterlich aus; er war aber alt und hatte einen langen weißen Bart.
sentences.l: Then a man stepped in, who was bigger than all the others, and looked frightful; but he was old and had a long white beard.
sentences.i: Then a man stepped in who was bigger than all the rest and looked frightful; but he was old, and had a long white beard.
sentences.n: “trat ... herein” — hereintreten, to step in. “sah ... aus” — aussehen, to look, appear (fürchterlich = frightful). Bart = beard, which becomes the boy’s handle on him.

sentences.t: „O du Wicht,“ rief er, „nun sollst du bald lernen was Gruseln ist, denn du sollst sterben.“
sentences.l: “O you wretch,” he cried, “now you shall soon learn what shuddering is, for you shall die.”
sentences.i: “O you wretch,” he cried, “now you shall soon learn what shuddering is, for you are to die.”
sentences.n: Wicht = a wretch, a poor or contemptible creature. “du sollst sterben” = ‘you are to die’ (sollen of decreed fate).

sentences.t: „Nicht so schnell,“ antwortete der Junge, „soll ich sterben, so muß ich auch dabei sein.“
sentences.l: “Not so fast,” answered the boy, “if I am to die, then I must be there too.”
sentences.i: “Not so fast,” answered the boy; “if I’m to die, I must have a hand in it too.”
sentences.n: “soll ich sterben, so ...” — a conditional made by fronting the verb (‘if I am to die, then ...’). “dabei sein” = to be present, be in on it — cheeky: he insists on a say in his own death.

sentences.t: „Dich will ich schon packen“ sprach der Unhold.
sentences.l: “You I will soon seize,” said the fiend.
sentences.i: “I’ll soon get hold of you,” said the fiend.
sentences.n: packen = to seize, grab. schon here = ‘soon enough, right enough.’ Unhold = a fiend, monster, ogre.

sentences.t: „Sachte, sachte, mach dich nicht so breit; so stark wie du bin ich auch, und wohl noch stärker.“
sentences.l: “Gently, gently, do not make yourself so broad; as strong as you am I too, and probably even stronger.”
sentences.i: “Gently, gently — don’t give yourself such airs; I’m as strong as you, and likely stronger still.”
sentences.n: sachte = gently, softly. “sich breit machen” = to spread oneself, to put on airs, to throw one’s weight about. stärker = stronger (comparative of stark).

sentences.t: „Das wollen wir sehn,“ sprach der Alte, „bist du stärker als ich, so will ich dich gehn lassen; komm, wir wollens versuchen.“
sentences.l: “That we shall see,” said the old man, “if you are stronger than I, then I will let you go; come, let us try it.”
sentences.i: “That we shall see,” said the old man; “if you’re stronger than I, I’ll let you go; come, let’s try it.”
sentences.n: sehn and gehn are contracted forms of sehen and gehen. der Alte = the old man (a nominalized adjective). “wir wollens versuchen” = ‘let us try it’ (wollens = wollen es).

sentences.t: Da führte er ihn durch dunkle Gänge zu einem Schmiedefeuer, nahm eine Axt und schlug den einen Amboß mit einem Schlag in die Erde.
sentences.l: Then he led him through dark passages to a smithy fire, took an axe and struck the one anvil with a single blow into the ground.
sentences.i: Then he led him through dark passages to a forge, took an axe, and with a single blow drove the one anvil into the ground.
sentences.n: Gänge = passages, corridors (Gang). Schmiedefeuer = a smithy fire, a forge. Amboß (1857 for Amboss) = anvil. “mit einem Schlag” = with one blow (Schlag).

sentences.t: „Das kann ich noch besser“ sprach der Junge, und gieng zu dem andern Amboß: der Alte stellte sich neben hin und wollte zusehen, und sein weißer Bart hieng herab.
sentences.l: “That I can do even better,” said the boy, and went to the other anvil: the old man placed himself beside it and wanted to watch, and his white beard hung down.
sentences.i: “I can do better than that,” said the boy, and went to the other anvil; the old man planted himself alongside to watch, and his white beard hung down.
sentences.n: “stellte sich neben hin” — sich hinstellen, to plant oneself (neben = beside). zusehen = to look on, to watch. hieng is the 1857 spelling of hing (‘hung’); “hieng herab” — herabhängen, to hang down. The dangling beard is about to be his undoing.

sentences.t: Da faßte der Junge die Axt, spaltete den Amboß auf einen Hieb und klemmte den Bart des Alten mit hinein.
sentences.l: Then the boy seized the axe, split the anvil at one stroke and jammed the old man’s beard in with it.
sentences.i: Then the boy seized the axe, split the anvil at a single stroke, and jammed the old man’s beard in with it.
sentences.n: spalten = to split, cleave. “auf einen Hieb” = at one stroke (Hieb = a blow, a cut). klemmen = to clamp, jam; “mit hinein” = in along with it (the beard caught in the split anvil).

sentences.t: „Nun hab ich dich,“ sprach der Junge, „jetzt ist das Sterben an dir.“
sentences.l: “Now I have you,” said the boy, “now the dying is upon you.”
sentences.i: “Now I’ve got you,” said the boy; “now it’s your turn to die.”
sentences.n: “jetzt ist das Sterben an dir” = ‘now it’s your turn to die’ (an jemandem sein = to be someone’s turn) — the boy throws the old man’s own threat back at him.

sentences.t: Dann faßte er eine Eisenstange und schlug auf den Alten los, bis er wimmerte und bat er möchte aufhören, er wollte ihm große Reichthümer geben.
sentences.l: Then he seized an iron bar and laid into the old man, until he whimpered and begged him to stop, saying he would give him great riches.
sentences.i: Then he took an iron bar and laid into the old man until he whimpered and begged him to stop, promising to give him great riches.
sentences.n: Eisenstange = an iron bar. “schlug ... los” — losschlagen, to lay into, strike at. wimmern = to whimper. “bat er möchte aufhören” — a reported plea in the subjunctive (‘begged that he would stop’; aufhören = to cease). Reichthümer (1857 for Reichtümer) = riches.

sentences.t: Der Junge zog die Axt raus, und ließ ihn los.
sentences.l: The boy pulled the axe out, and let him loose.
sentences.i: The boy pulled the axe out and let him go.
sentences.n: “zog ... raus” — herausziehen (colloquial raus), to pull out. “ließ ihn los” — loslassen, to let go, release.

sentences.t: Der Alte führte ihn wieder ins Schloß zurück und zeigte ihm in einem Keller drei Kasten voll Gold.
sentences.l: The old man led him back into the castle again and showed him three chests full of gold in a cellar.
sentences.i: The old man led him back into the castle and showed him three chests full of gold in a cellar.
sentences.n: “führte ... zurück” — zurückführen, to lead back. Keller = cellar. Kasten = a chest, a box (here plural). This is the treasure the innkeeper had spoken of.

sentences.t: „Davon,“ sprach er, „ist ein Theil den Armen, der andere dem König, der dritte dein.“
sentences.l: “Of this,” he said, “one part is for the poor, the second for the king, the third yours.”
sentences.i: “Of this,” he said, “one part is for the poor, another for the king, and the third is yours.”
sentences.n: “davon” = of it. Theil (1857 for Teil) = part. Note the datives den Armen (‘for the poor’) and dem König (‘for the king’), marking the recipients.

sentences.t: Indem schlug es zwölfe, und der Geist verschwand, also daß der Junge im finstern stand.
sentences.l: At that moment it struck twelve, and the spirit vanished, so that the boy stood in the dark.
sentences.i: At that moment the clock struck twelve, and the spirit vanished, so that the boy stood in the dark.
sentences.n: indem here = ‘just then, at that moment.’ “schlug es zwölfe” = ‘it struck twelve’ (zwölfe, an old inflected ‘twelve’). Geist = spirit, ghost. “also daß” = so that. “im finstern” = in the dark (finster = dark).

sentences.t: „Ich werde mir doch heraushelfen können“ sprach er, tappte herum, fand den Weg in die Kammer und schlief dort bei seinem Feuer ein.
sentences.l: “I shall surely be able to help myself out,” he said, groped about, found the way to the chamber and fell asleep there by his fire.
sentences.i: “I’ll manage to find my own way out,” he said, groped about, found the way to the chamber, and fell asleep there by his fire.
sentences.n: “sich heraushelfen” = to help oneself out (heraushelfen, separable). tappen = to grope, fumble (in the dark). “schlief ... ein” — einschlafen.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen kam der König und sagte „nun wirst du gelernt haben was Gruseln ist?“
sentences.l: The next morning the king came and said, “now you will have learned what shuddering is?”
sentences.i: The next morning the king came and said, “Now surely you will have learned what shuddering is?”
sentences.n: “nun wirst du gelernt haben” — the future perfect used as a confident guess (‘you must have learned by now’). gelernt = learned.

sentences.t: „Nein,“ antwortete er, „was ists nur? mein todter Vetter war da, und ein bärtiger Mann ist gekommen, der hat mir da unten viel Geld gezeigt, aber was Gruseln ist hat mir keiner gesagt.“
sentences.l: “No,” he answered, “what ever is it? my dead cousin was here, and a bearded man came, who showed me much money down below, but what shuddering is no one has told me.”
sentences.i: “No,” he answered; “what ever can it be? My dead cousin was here, and a bearded man came who showed me a great deal of money down below — but what shuddering is, no one has told me.”
sentences.n: “was ists nur” = ‘what ever is it?’ (ists = ist es; nur intensifies the puzzlement). todter Vetter = dead cousin (todt, 1857). bärtig = bearded. gezeigt = shown. “hat mir keiner gesagt” = ‘no one has told me’ (keiner = no one).

sentences.t: Da sprach der König „du hast das Schloß erlöst und sollst meine Tochter heirathen.“
sentences.l: Then the king said, “you have redeemed the castle and shall marry my daughter.”
sentences.i: Then the king said, “You have freed the castle from its curse, and shall marry my daughter.”
sentences.n: erlösen = to redeem, deliver, break the spell on. heirathen is the 1857 spelling of heiraten (‘to marry’), with the old -th-.

sentences.t: „Das ist all recht gut,“ antwortete er, „aber ich weiß noch immer nicht was Gruseln ist.“
sentences.l: “That is all very well,” he answered, “but I still do not know what shuddering is.”
sentences.i: “That’s all very well,” he answered, “but I still don’t know what shuddering is.”
sentences.n: “das ist all recht gut” = ‘that’s all very well.’ “noch immer nicht” = still not. His one grievance survives even the winning of a kingdom.

sentences.t: Da ward das Gold herauf gebracht und die Hochzeit gefeiert, aber der junge König, so lieb er seine Gemahlin hatte und so vergnügt er war, sagte doch immer „wenn mir nur gruselte, wenn mir nur gruselte.“
sentences.l: Then the gold was brought up and the wedding celebrated, but the young king, however dearly he loved his consort and however happy he was, still kept saying, “if only it shuddered me, if only it shuddered me.”
sentences.i: Then the gold was brought up and the wedding held, but the young king, however dearly he loved his consort and however happy he was, still kept saying, “If only I could shudder, if only I could shudder.”
sentences.n: ward — the archaic preterite of werden (here the passive ‘was brought’). Gemahlin = consort, wedded wife (elevated). “so lieb er ... hatte ... so vergnügt er war” = ‘however dearly he loved ... however content he was’ (a concessive so ... so). vergnügt = content, merry.

sentences.t: Das verdroß sie endlich.
sentences.l: That vexed her at last.
sentences.n: verdrießen = to vex, annoy (verdroß, the preterite; note the ß). ‘She’ is the young queen, worn out by his one endless complaint.

sentences.t: Ihr Kammermädchen sprach „ich will Hilfe schaffen, das Gruseln soll er schon lernen.“
sentences.l: Her chambermaid said, “I will bring help; the shuddering he shall learn right enough.”
sentences.i: Her chambermaid said, “I’ll see it put right — he shall learn to shudder yet.”
sentences.n: Kammermädchen = a chambermaid, a lady’s maid. “Hilfe schaffen” = to bring, provide help. “das Gruseln soll er schon lernen” — the confident schon (‘right enough, yet’) echoes the father’s promise at the tale’s start.

sentences.t: Sie gieng hinaus zum Bach, der durch den Garten floß, und ließ sich einen ganzen Eimer voll Gründlinge holen.
sentences.l: She went out to the brook that flowed through the garden, and had a whole bucketful of gudgeons fetched for herself.
sentences.i: She went out to the brook that ran through the garden, and had a whole bucketful of gudgeons fetched.
sentences.n: der Bach = brook; floß = flowed (fließen). “ließ sich ... holen” = had (them) fetched for herself (lassen, causative). Eimer = bucket. Gründlinge = gudgeons, little bottom-dwelling freshwater fish (singular Gründling) — the tale’s homely instrument of terror.

sentences.t: Nachts, als der junge König schlief, mußte seine Gemahlin ihm die Decke wegziehen und den Eimer voll kalt Wasser mit den Gründlingen über ihn herschütten, daß die kleinen Fische um ihn herum zappelten.
sentences.l: At night, when the young king slept, his consort had to pull the cover off him and pour the bucket full of cold water with the gudgeons over him, so that the little fish wriggled all around him.
sentences.i: At night, while the young king slept, his consort had to pull the cover off him and tip the bucket of cold water and gudgeons over him, so that the little fish wriggled all around him.
sentences.n: “die Decke wegziehen” — wegziehen (separable), to pull away, with the dative ihm (‘off him’). “kalt Wasser” keeps the old uninflected adjective (modern kaltes Wasser). herschütten = to pour or tip over (toward, i.e. onto him). Fische = fish (plural); zappeln = to wriggle, flounder — the very word used earlier of the hanged men, now of the fish.

sentences.t: Da wachte er auf und rief „ach was gruselt mir, was gruselt mir, liebe Frau!
sentences.l: Then he woke up and cried, “oh, how it shudders me, how it shudders me, dear wife!
sentences.i: Then he woke up and cried, “Oh, how it makes me shudder, how it makes me shudder, dear wife!
sentences.n: “wachte ... auf” — aufwachen, to wake up. “was gruselt mir” = ‘how it gives me the creeps!’ (was here = ‘how much’; the impersonal gruselt with dative mir). At last, from cold water and wriggling fish, the shudder comes.

sentences.t: Ja, nun weiß ich was Gruseln ist.“
sentences.l: Yes, now I know what shuddering is.”
sentences.i: Yes, now I know what shuddering is!”
sentences.n: The tale’s punch-line: the fearless boy learns to shudder not from ghosts, corpses or a giant, but from a bucketful of cold water and little fish in his bed.
